questions.verifiedExplanation: The defining distinction between hypertensive emergency and urgency is the presence of acute end-organ damage, NOT a specific BP threshold. Hypertensive emergency = severely elevated BP with evidence of acute target-organ injury (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, pulmonary edema, aortic dissection, AKI, eclampsia). Hypertensive urgency = severely elevated BP without end-organ damage. The BP number alone does not determine classification — patients with chronic uncontrolled HTN may tolerate very high pressures without acute injury, while previously normotensive patients (e.g., eclampsia) may develop emergencies at lower BP levels. As StatPearls emphasizes: 'treat the patient, not the number.' Emergency requires IV antihypertensives and ICU admission; urgency requires oral medications and outpatient follow-up.
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

Ahmed I, Alley WD, Chauhan S, Afzal M. Hypertensive Crisis. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK507701/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: A hypertensive emergency is an acute, marked elevation in blood pressure that is associated with signs of target-organ damage

supporting.keyFinding: Urgencies involve severe BP elevation without evidence of acute target-organ damage; emergencies are associated with life-threatening organ injury

questions.verifiedExplanation: In most hypertensive emergencies, BP should be reduced gradually: MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour, then to approximately 160/100 mmHg over 2-6 hours, with normalization over 24-48 hours. This stepwise approach prevents iatrogenic hypoperfusion — patients with chronic HTN have rightward-shifted cerebral autoregulation curves, so rapid normalization can cause watershed infarction. The key exception is aortic dissection, which requires more aggressive lowering (SBP <120 within minutes). IV agents must be rapidly titratable — oral agents like clonidine and sublingual nifedipine have no role in acute management because their effects are unpredictable and not easily reversed.
verifiedReferences: Potter T, Agarwal A, Schaefer TJ. Hypertensive Encephalopathy. [Updated 2024 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554499/ [Open Access]

Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: reduce systolic blood pressure by no more than 25% within the initial hour, followed by a gradual blood pressure reduction to 160/100 mm Hg over the next 2 to 6 hours

supporting.keyFinding: lower the mean arterial pressure by 20% to 25% within the first 1 to 2 hours

questions.verifiedExplanation: Aortic dissection is the critical exception to the gradual 25% MAP reduction rule. The target is SBP <120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm within 20 minutes using IV beta-blockers (esmolol or labetalol) as first-line. Beta-blockers reduce both BP and the rate of aortic pressure rise (dP/dt), decreasing shear stress on the intimal flap. If beta-blockers alone are insufficient, add IV vasodilators (nicardipine, nitroprusside) AFTER adequate heart rate control — vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia, which worsens dissection propagation. The standard 25% MAP reduction would be dangerously inadequate because continued hypertension and tachycardia drive ongoing dissection extension, aortic rupture, and death.
verifiedReferences: Potter T, Agarwal A, Schaefer TJ. Hypertensive Encephalopathy. [Updated 2024 Feb 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554499/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: Exceptions to conservative blood pressure reduction include...aortic dissection

questions.verifiedExplanation: PRES classically shows bilateral parieto-occipital vasogenic edema on MRI, appearing as hyperintense signals on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. The posterior predominance is explained by the sparser sympathetic innervation of the posterior cerebral circulation compared to the anterior (carotid) circulation, making it more vulnerable to autoregulatory breakthrough during acute hypertension. Key MRI features: vasogenic edema (increased ADC, distinguishing from cytotoxic edema/infarction). PRES spares the calcarine cortex, differentiating it from PCA stroke. Atypical regions (frontal lobes, cerebellum, brainstem, basal ganglia) occur in up to 30% of cases. PRES is usually reversible with prompt BP control and treatment of the underlying cause (hypertension, eclampsia, immunosuppressants), but delayed treatment can lead to irreversible infarction.
verifiedReferences: Zelaya JE, Al-Khoury L. Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome. [Updated 2024 Oct 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554492/ [Open Access]

primary.supportingQuote: classically diagnosed based on MRI imaging most commonly seen in the parieto-occipital lobes...vasogenic edema hyperintense on T2 with FLAIR sequences

issuesFound: CRITICAL: Answer truncated to 'Bilateral parieto' — fix to 'Bilateral parieto-occipital white matter edema'

questions.verifiedExplanation: Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine-1 (D1) receptor agonist that simultaneously lowers BP and increases renal blood flow, GFR, and natriuresis — making it the preferred choice for hypertensive emergency complicated by AKI. Its renal-protective mechanism (D1-mediated renal arteriolar vasodilation) is unique among IV antihypertensives. Nitroprusside should be avoided in renal impairment because thiocyanate (its metabolite) accumulates with reduced renal clearance, causing neurotoxicity. Hydralazine is a direct arteriolar vasodilator but has unpredictable onset and duration, is poorly titratable, and causes reflex tachycardia. Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting beta-blocker used for dissection and perioperative HTN but may reduce renal perfusion and lacks renal-protective properties.
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

questions.verifiedExplanation: This presentation — BP ≥160/110, headache, and proteinuria at 36 weeks — defines severe preeclampsia. First-line antihypertensives are IV labetalol or IV hydralazine, both safe in pregnancy. Magnesium sulfate should be started concurrently for seizure prophylaxis (it is NOT an antihypertensive). Critical distractor analysis: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) are absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy — they cause fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, and neonatal death. This is NOT eclampsia, which requires seizures (headache alone is insufficient). For eclamptic seizures, magnesium sulfate is first-line, NOT diazepam. Simply continuing home medications ignores the new-onset proteinuria and symptoms indicating preeclampsia. Definitive treatment is delivery, and at ≥37 weeks (or 36 weeks with severe features), delivery should be expedited after maternal stabilization.
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

supporting.keyFinding: Blood pressure control in pregnancy; seizures warrant immediate delivery

questions.verifiedExplanation: Sodium nitroprusside contains five cyanide (CN) groups per molecule. During metabolism, cyanide is released and normally converted to thiocyanate by hepatic rhodanese, then excreted renally. With prolonged infusion (>48-72 hours) or high doses (>2 mcg/kg/min), cyanide production exceeds detoxification capacity, causing cyanide toxicity — which inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV), blocking mitochondrial aerobic metabolism and causing lactic acidosis. Clinical signs include tachyphylaxis (rising BP despite increasing dose), altered mental status, seizures, and cardiovascular collapse. Thiocyanate toxicity (distinct from cyanide) can additionally occur in renal failure. Treatment: sodium thiosulfate + hydroxocobalamin. Due to these risks, nitroprusside has been largely replaced by safer agents (nicardipine, clevidipine).
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

questions.verifiedExplanation: Sublingual nifedipine (a short-acting dihydropyridine CCB) is explicitly contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies because it causes rapid, unpredictable, and uncontrollable BP drops that have been associated with stroke, MI, and death. Unlike IV agents, sublingual nifedipine cannot be titrated — once administered, the precipitous vasodilation cannot be reversed. The rapid BP drop can fall below the cerebral autoregulatory range (rightward-shifted in chronically hypertensive patients), causing watershed infarcts. This led to an regulatory advisory against the practice. StatPearls states that 'oral medications, such as clonidine and nifedipine, play no role in the immediate management of a hypertensive emergency.' The key principle: agents for HTN emergency must be both rapidly acting AND easily titratable, which requires IV administration with continuous monitoring.
verifiedReferences: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]
